name: 1fichier
summary: Long-running freemium cyberlocker with guest uploads, registered file management, official API support, and sharply tiered free-versus-premium download rules.

name: AndroidFileHost
summary: Large Android-focused file hosting network for developers with unlimited downloads, multiple mirrors, and a public beta API.

free_model.notes: This candidate has previously been verified for a free, guest, or free-account path. Recheck first-party sources before publishing or editing the row.

max_file_size.notes: Max file size is conditional. Android File Host does not publish a formal general per-file upload cap for the free tier, but current public file listings include downloads up to at least 1.6 GB, so practical upload size clearly exceeds smaller ROM-sized files and may vary by device or project listing.

retention.notes: Retention depends on download activity. The terms say Android File Host generally will not delete uploaded files, but it may remove files with fewer than 10 downloads over 365 consecutive days.

storage.notes: Storage is conditional. Android File Host says it does not limit the number of files, but the terms still reserve the right to suspend service for excessive storage use, so no clean byte quota is published.

bandwidth.notes: Android File Host markets unlimited bandwidth and download speeds in its "Unlimited Everything" section, while the terms still reserve the right to suspend service for excessive bandwidth use.

account.benefits: Developers need an account for uploads and account-managed services; visitors can still browse device pages and download public files without registering.

developer.notes: Android File Host publishes a beta API for browsing devices, developers, OTA update checks, folders, and files; direct-download URLs require a beta API key from the user dashboard.

security.notes: The site runs over HTTPS and provides hosted storage, but the public pages do not claim end-to-end encryption or promise that stored files are encrypted for you.

name: Baidu Pan
summary: Baidu's large-scale cloud storage service with multi-device backup, file sharing, iPhone/Mac apps, an official PCS open platform, and very large paid expansion tiers.

free_model.notes: This rejected candidate is not classified for free-model display; see the rejection reason for the review outcome.

max_file_size.notes: Baidu's official help center says ordinary users can upload up to 1 GB per file on the web and up to 4 GB per file in the Windows and Mac desktop clients. Paid members can upload up to 10 GB per file in the desktop clients, and super members up to 20 GB.

max_file_size_account.notes: Baidu's official help center says ordinary account holders can upload up to 1 GB per file on the web and up to 4 GB per file in the Windows and Mac desktop clients. Paid members can upload up to 10 GB per file in the desktop clients, and super members up to 20 GB.

retention.notes: Files remain until deleted. Baidu's official help center says deleted files stay in the recycle bin for 10 days on ordinary accounts, 15 days for members, and 30 days for super members before permanent deletion.

storage.notes: The cited first-party pages still do not cleanly expose one stable default free-storage quota in accessible text. Current official help says every account has a free quota, mobile-app login can grant surprise bonus capacity, and some promotional capacity can expire, but it still does not publish one reliable base free number we can promote into the dataset.

bandwidth.notes: Bandwidth is conditional. Baidu's current official help says the Mac client does not impose upload or download speed throttling, but the cited first-party pages still do not publish one clean universal free-user traffic quota across web, desktop, and sharing flows.

account.benefits: A Baidu account is required for cloud backup, syncing, link sharing, and API-authorized access across devices, but current user-provided tests indicate the registration and SMS-login flows are not working reliably for Bangladeshi phone numbers.

developer.notes: Baidu publishes an official PCS open platform for personal cloud storage with file APIs, structured-data APIs, OAuth-based access, and SDK downloads, but the cited official pages still do not present a simple end-user CLI workflow.

security.notes: The App Store listing says the service uses multiple data centers and cites ISO27001 and ISO27018 security certifications. The official help center also says users can protect shared links with passwords and set automatic link expiry, but it still does not claim end-to-end encryption.

name: Buzzheavier
summary: Anonymous file-sharing host with no free file-size cap, curl-friendly APIs, and activity-based retention that can become effectively permanent.

free_model.notes: This candidate has previously been verified for a free, guest, or free-account path. Recheck first-party sources before publishing or editing the row.

retention.notes: Retention is conditional. Free files start at 15 days, each download adds 3 days up to 45 days, and files that get at least 15 downloads every 45 days live forever.

storage.notes: Storage is conditional. Buzzheavier's official help, pricing, and blog frame the free tier around activity-based file lifetime rather than a reusable personal quota: free files expire unless they keep getting downloaded, while paid plans sell permanent storage by the terabyte.

bandwidth.notes: Unlimited. Buzzheavier says there are no bandwidth limits and no speed throttling.

account.benefits: Anonymous uploads work without an account. Accounts add an account ID, file-manager APIs, webhook support, and organized storage locations.

security.notes: The service runs over HTTPS. The help page says it does not log IPs or track who uploads or downloads what, but it does not claim end-to-end encryption.

name: Cloudflare R2

name: Disroot Upload
summary: Privacy-focused temporary file sharing from Disroot, powered by Lufi and encrypted in the browser before upload.

free_model.notes: This candidate has previously been verified for a free, guest, or free-account path. Recheck first-party sources before publishing or editing the row.

max_file_size.notes: Maximum upload behavior is conditional. The current hosted upload page does not publish one hard maximum file size, but it does expose size-based retention rules for the hosted instance, including 0-96 MB, 96-954 MB, and 954 MB or larger files.

retention.notes: Retention is conditional. The current upload page lets users choose 24 hours, 7 days, 30 days, or 90 days, but the actual expiry becomes the smaller of the chosen delay and the instance's size-based server limits: 0-96 MB files are kept 90 days, 96-954 MB files are kept 15 days, and 954 MB or larger files are kept 2 days.

storage.notes: Disroot Upload is a temporary sharing service rather than persistent account storage. The official service page frames it as temporary encrypted file sharing with download and deletion links, not as a reusable cloud drive quota.

bandwidth.notes: Bandwidth is conditional. The official Disroot and hosted Lufi pages do not publish a numeric throughput cap, and effective transfer availability depends on the sender's chosen delay, the optional delete-at-first-download mode, and the instance's size-based retention limits.

account.benefits: No account is needed to upload files; users receive download and deletion links.

developer.notes: The cited current public pages do not document a public API or CLI workflow for this hosted instance.

security.notes: Disroot states files are encrypted in the browser before upload, so administrators cannot see the file contents.

name: Fileditch
summary: Anonymous file-sharing host with a no-auth HTTP API, 25 GB files, and low-disk cleanup only after 30 days of inactivity.

free_model.notes: This candidate has previously been verified for a free, guest, or free-account path. Recheck first-party sources before publishing or editing the row.

max_file_size.notes: The FAQ and API docs both state a 25 GB maximum file size.

retention.notes: Retention is conditional. FileDitch keeps files indefinitely while storage allows, and only removes the oldest files that have not been accessed in 30 days when disk space is genuinely needed.

storage.notes: Storage is conditional. The FAQ says there is no limit on the number of files you can upload, but files are only kept while capacity allows and inactive files can be reclaimed when disk space is genuinely needed.

bandwidth.notes: Bandwidth is conditional. FileDitch does not publish a transfer-volume allowance, but the FAQ says uploads are protected by rate limiting and automated abuse prevention, and hotlinking or embedding from external sites is not supported.

account.benefits: No account is required. FileDitch is fully anonymous and does not offer login-based upload tracking.

developer.notes: FileDitch documents simple no-auth HTTP uploads with curl, raw POST uploads, multipart uploads, and JSON responses.

security.notes: The public pages use HTTPS and say uploaded links are unlisted, but they explicitly say privacy is not guaranteed and do not claim end-to-end encryption.

sources.label: Homepage
sources.notes: Current host surface for the active FileDitch instance.

sources.label: FAQ
sources.notes: Current FAQ documents indefinite retention while storage allows, 30-day inactivity cleanup when space is needed, anonymous uploads, and blocked file types.

sources.label: API
sources.notes: Current API docs document no-auth HTTP uploads, curl examples, JSON responses, blocked file types, and a 25 GB file-size limit.

allowed_file_types.notes: FileDitch allows most common file types but blocks executables, installers, scripts, web files, macro-enabled Office files, Java archives, and other risky server-executable content.

public_sharing.notes: Public link or share-page support is part of this verified candidate record; see the cited source notes for the current sharing surface.

name: FileFast
summary: Modern file-sharing and cloud-storage service with guest uploads, file analytics, public API access, and a free tier with monthly bandwidth limits.

free_model.notes: This candidate has previously been verified for a free, guest, or free-account path. Recheck first-party sources before publishing or editing the row.

max_file_size.notes: The visible web uploader and homepage currently advertise a 6 GB maximum upload size. The FAQ still says up to 5 GB per transfer, while the API docs cap authenticated large uploads at 5 GB, so the larger 6 GB figure is treated as the current browser-based guest/web flow rather than a universal API ceiling.

retention.notes: The premium page says free-plan files expire after 7 days of inactivity. The FAQ also describes automatic expiration for free storage, though it does not repeat the exact number.

storage.notes: Storage is conditional. FileFast's first-party pages conflict on free storage: the FAQ says 10 GB with automatic expiration, while the premium page says 100 GB storage on the free plan. The dataset leaves the clean numeric quota unpublished until the official surfaces agree.

bandwidth.notes: Both the FAQ and premium page currently describe 100 GB monthly bandwidth on the free plan.

max_file_size_guest.notes: The visible guest/web upload flow currently shows a 6 GB maximum upload file size and expiry choices including keeping files until 7 days of inactivity.

max_file_size_account.notes: The API docs require authentication and explicitly support large file uploads up to 5 GB.

storage_account.notes: Storage is conditional. The first-party pages conflict between 10 GB and 100 GB of free storage, so the dataset leaves the account storage quota unpublished pending a cleaner official statement.

account.benefits: Guest uploads are supported from the web interface, while creating an account unlocks file management, statistics, folders, password protection, and API token access.

developer.notes: FileFast publishes API documentation for uploads, large presigned uploads, file info, file listing, deletion, and folders.

security.notes: FileFast describes industry-standard encryption, TLS1.2+, server-side encryption, and user-controlled privacy settings, but it does not claim end-to-end encryption by default.

name: FileGarden
summary: Persistent file-linking service that lets users upload files for long-lived sharing, with active user gardens and lightweight account features.

free_model.notes: This candidate has previously been verified for a free, guest, or free-account path. Recheck first-party sources before publishing or editing the row.

max_file_size.notes: The current public File Garden pipe interface rejects files above 100 MiB in its live embedded JavaScript, with an explicit error message saying uploads above 100 MiB are not supported.

max_file_size_guest.notes: The current public File Garden pipe interface is openly accessible and rejects files above 100 MiB with an explicit client-side error message.

max_file_size_account.notes: The accessible official surfaces do not publish a different account-side free upload cap from the live public pipe interface, so the current documented 100 MiB limit is used for signed-in uploads as well.

retention.notes: No automatic expiry. The current homepage says File Garden lets you upload any type of file to link elsewhere forever for free.

storage.notes: Storage is conditional. File Garden clearly positions itself as a persistent file-linking service where uploads can live forever for free, but the accessible official surfaces still do not publish a reusable personal storage quota.

storage_guest.notes: Storage is conditional for public/guest-facing uploads. File Garden presents files as living forever for free, but the accessible official surfaces do not publish a reusable guest storage quota and instead expose sharing-oriented public pipe flows.

storage_account.notes: Storage is conditional for signed-in users too. File Garden clearly has account-linked gardens and login flows, but the accessible official surfaces still do not publish a clean numeric personal storage quota.

bandwidth.notes: Bandwidth is conditional. File Garden does not publish a numeric throughput cap, and the live first-party frontend makes transfer accessibility depend on privacy mode (public, unlisted, private) and signed-in visibility, warning unsigned viewers that not all items may be visible.

account.benefits: The current public surface clearly includes login and sign-up flows plus persistent user gardens, but the accessible official snippets do not cleanly spell out whether every upload path requires an account.

developer.notes: The accessible public surfaces do not document a public API or CLI workflow.

security.notes: File Garden runs over HTTPS, but the accessible public pages do not claim end-to-end encryption.

name: Gofile

retention.notes: pCloud does not publish an automatic expiry for standard stored files; files remain until the user deletes them, after which free-plan files stay in Trash for 15 days and paid-plan files for 30 days.

storage.notes: pCloud's free Basic plan offers up to 10 GB, with 2 GB available at signup and the remainder unlocked through onboarding or referrals.

bandwidth.notes: pCloud Basic currently includes 50 GB per month of shared-link traffic; upload traffic is limited to up to twice the account storage within 30 days.

developer.notes: pCloud publishes official developer documentation, SDK references, and HTTP/JSON API docs.

security.notes: The cited app/download pages describe an optional zero-knowledge encrypted Crypto area rather than default end-to-end encryption for the whole account.

name: Pillowcase

name: PutRe
summary: Anonymous file-sharing service with browser uploads, direct links, optional albums, and a public API.

free_model.notes: This candidate has previously been verified for a free, guest, or free-account path. Recheck first-party sources before publishing or editing the row.

max_file_size.notes: The official features page says the web interface supports uploads up to 200 MB, while the documented HTTP API examples describe a 100 MB API limit.

retention.notes: Files remain until deleted by the uploader or removed for terms-of-service violations.

storage.notes: Storage is conditional. put.re exposes anonymous uploads and optional album-style management rather than a published reusable storage allowance, and its official feature pages only promise that files remain until deleted or removed for terms violations.

bandwidth.notes: Bandwidth is conditional. put.re does not publish numeric transfer quotas, and the official feature pages only promise CDN-backed delivery, direct links, and fast access rather than a fixed bandwidth allowance.

max_file_size_guest.notes: Anonymous browser uploads work without an account and are capped at 200 MB. The uploader source switches from plain HTTP to WebSockets above 50 MB.

storage_guest.notes: Storage is conditional. Anonymous uploads are supported and can optionally be organized through album-style management flows, but the official pages do not publish a reusable guest-storage quota.

account.benefits: No account is required. Uploads return delete links, and the public API also supports album creation with an ID and secret for later editing.

developer.notes: The public API docs include curl examples for file upload, deletion, metadata lookup, album creation, health checks, and removal requests.

security.notes: The features page says files are stored encrypted in Amazon S3, never written to disk unencrypted, and are served over HTTPS. The service does not claim end-to-end encryption.

name: Stashr
summary: Guest-friendly file-sharing host with 500 MB files, selectable expiry or download limits, and optional client-side encryption.

free_model.notes: This candidate has previously been verified for a free, guest, or free-account path. Recheck first-party sources before publishing or editing the row.

max_file_size.notes: The homepage and FAQ state a 500 MB maximum file size.

max_file_size_account.notes: Free accounts add a dashboard and file management, but the current homepage and FAQ do not publish a separate higher free account cap beyond the same 500 MB file-size limit.

retention.notes: Retention is conditional. Uploads can be set to 1 hour, 6 hours, 12 hours, 1 day, 7 days, 30 days, or forever. The FAQ says files are stored indefinitely by default, while the terms describe 30 days if no expiration time is set.

storage.notes: Storage is conditional. Stashr stores files permanently by default unless the uploader chooses an expiration or download limit, but anonymous uploads are not tied to a reusable personal storage quota and the public pages do not publish one.

bandwidth.notes: Unlimited. The FAQ says all users get maximum upload and download speeds, with no throttling and no premium tiers. The service still applies per-IP upload rate limits and abuse-prevention controls.

account.benefits: No account is required for basic uploads. Free accounts add a dashboard, file management, and download statistics.

developer.notes: The cited public pages do not document a public API or CLI workflow.

security.notes: Stashr offers optional client-side AES-256-GCM encryption before upload. Password protection is separate from encryption, and the public pages do not claim that all files are end-to-end encrypted by default.

sources.notes: Current homepage shows 500 MB files, optional client-side encryption, password protection, download limits, and selectable storage durations.

security.notes: The service uses HTTPS, but the cited public pages do not claim end-to-end encryption.

name: WdFiles
summary: Active Russian cyberlocker whose official FAQ and premium page expose clear registered and premium limits, but whose public warez-style indexing keeps it out of the verified list.

free_model.notes: This rejected candidate is not classified for free-model display; see the rejection reason for the review outcome.

max_file_size.notes: Conditional: the current homepage upload widget shows a 3 GB upload limit, while the official FAQ says free and normal accounts can upload files up to 6 GB each.

retention.notes: The official FAQ says guest files are stored 120 days after last download, registered-user files 360 days, and premium-account files for an unlimited period.

storage.notes: The official FAQ only publishes a dedicated storage allocation for registered users, not for anonymous uploads.

bandwidth.notes: Conditional: the homepage and premium page say premium removes speed limits, waiting, and download restrictions, but they do not publish one clean free-tier traffic allowance.

max_file_size_account.notes: The official FAQ says each uploaded file for free and normal accounts must not exceed 6 GB, while premium accounts can go up to 10 GB.

storage_account.notes: The official FAQ says each registered user gets 250 GB for free, while the premium page says premium storage becomes unlimited.

account.benefits: Guest uploads exist, while registered users get 250 GB for free, file retention up to 360 days, and a documented 6 GB per-file limit before premium raises storage to unlimited and per-file upload to 10 GB.

developer.notes: No public API or CLI workflow was surfaced in the accessible official WDfiles pages reviewed, though the homepage does advertise remote upload from external sites.

security.notes: The reviewed official pages are served over HTTPS, but no end-to-end or server-side encryption guarantees were surfaced in the accessible official materials.

name: Yandex Disk
summary: Cloud storage service with a 5 GB base tier, official desktop and mobile apps, and a Linux console client.

free_model.notes: This candidate has previously been verified for a free, guest, or free-account path. Recheck first-party sources before publishing or editing the row.

max_file_size.notes: Without an active Yandex 360 plan, the maximum size of a single uploaded file is currently 1 GB.

retention.notes: Files remain until deleted. Yandex says files are stored until you delete them if there are no access issues, with 30 days of Trash recovery, while permanent deletion can still happen after 2 years of not using Yandex Disk or after a space-overage block persists for 90 days.

storage.notes: Current public help pages use 5 GB as the base Yandex Disk capacity in their examples.

bandwidth.notes: Yandex limits uploads to twice the storage capacity of the current plan each month. With the 5 GB base plan, that works out to 10 GB of uploads per month before uploads are temporarily restricted.

account.benefits: A Yandex account is required to use Yandex Disk storage, sharing, desktop sync, and mobile apps.

developer.notes: The cited current public pages describe official apps and a Linux console client, but they do not document a public developer API.

security.notes: The cited help pages do not claim end-to-end encryption for standard Yandex Disk storage.
